summary: Joel briefly promotes StackOverflow Podcast episode 19, where he and Jeff Atwood discussed the design of StackOverflow's home page and voting mechanics. They also discussed Aaron Swartz's article on software launches, contrasting big-bang launches (like Cuil's disastrous debut) with quiet, incremental rollouts (like Gmail). Joel wonders whether StackOverflow's own launch will overwhelm their servers while underwhelming their audience.
key_points: The StackOverflow home page design and question voting semantics were active design decisions being worked through
Big-bang product launches risk disaster, as demonstrated by Cuil's failed launch
Quiet, gradual launches that build momentum over time can be more effective, as Gmail demonstrated
The dual fear of any launch: technical infrastructure failing under load while simultaneously failing to generate audience excitement
money_quotes: Big-bang launches can be disasters (viz.: cuil); quiet, gentle launches without announcements where you slowly build can work a lot better (viz.: Gmail).
Will StackOverflow's launch overwhelm our servers and underwhelm our audience?
key_insight: Gradual, quiet software launches that build organically tend to succeed more reliably than high-pressure big-bang debuts.
